flashcards.front: Define Binary Search and explain its core principle.
flashcards.back: Binary Search is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that finds the position of a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. If the target value is less than the middle element, the search continues in the left half; otherwise, it continues in the right half.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Binary Search? Derive the recurrence relation.
flashcards.back: Binary Search has a time complexity of O(log n). The recurrence relation is T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which solves to T(n) = O(log n) by applying the Master Theorem (a(n^b) where a=1, b=1, so log_b(a)=0).

flashcards.front: Write the Iterative implementation of Binary Search in Python.
Write the Recursive implementation of Binary Search in Python.
How do you find the FIRST occurrence of a target value in a sorted array with duplicates using Binary Search?
flashcards.back: Modify standard binary search to continue searching in the left half even after finding a match. Return the index when arr[mid] == target and (mid == 0 or arr[mid-1] < target). Time complexity remains O(log n).

flashcards.front: How do you find the LAST occurrence of a target value in a sorted array with duplicates using Binary Search?
flashcards.back: Similar to first occurrence, but continue searching the right half after finding a match. Return the index when arr[mid] == target and (mid == n-1 or arr[mid+1] > target). This allows counting total occurrences as (last - first + 1).

flashcards.front: What are the key edge cases and pitfalls in Binary Search implementation?
flashcards.back: Key edge cases: (1) Empty array returns -1, (2) Target not found returns -1, (3) Single element array, (4) Target at first or last position. Pitfalls: (1) Integer overflow in mid calculation (use left + (right-left)//2), (2) Using <= vs < incorrectly causing missed elements.

flashcards.front: Compare Linear Search and Binary Search with respect to prerequisites, time complexity, and suitable use cases.
flashcards.back: Linear Search: O(n) time, works on sorted/unsorted arrays, no prerequisites. Binary Search: O(log n) time, requires sorted array, needs random access. Use Linear Search for small arrays or unsorted data; use Binary Search for large sorted datasets or frequent queries.
